execution_strategy:
Execute as 4 phases in dependency order. Each phase is independently
testable and committable; the version bump (v0.104.1 → v0.105.0) lands
only after all four phases are completed.

phases[0] - --ack-only (P1)
Smallest blast radius: one function (EmitMutationJson) edit plus a
shared BaseOptions flag plumbed through the 12 call sites. Lands
the new schema constant (kMutationAckSchema =
"uni-plan-mutation-ack-v1") in UniPlanCliConstants.h. Tests cover
(a) the shape difference under --ack-only vs default, (b) on-disk
bundle content is byte-identical regardless of the flag,
(c) auto-changelog behavior unchanged, (d) exit codes unchanged.
Delivers the largest token-savings impact in the topic.

phases[1] - --all-phases canonicalization (P7)
Adds --all-phases sugar + phase readiness batch loop. No mutation
path touched. Adds a new response schema uni-plan-phase-readiness-
batch-v1 paralleling the existing uni-plan-phase-readiness-v1 for
single-phase invocations. phase metric and phase get keep
accepting --phases <csv>; --all-phases is additive sugar that
expands to "0..N-1". Tests cover the batch shape + equivalence
with the enumerated-csv form + empty-phases and single-phase
edge cases.

phases[2] - task set --description + gate (P8)
Option-struct addition + parser extension + mutation handler gate.
FTaskSetOptions gains mDescription + mDescriptionFile + mbForce +
mReason fields; ParseTaskSetOptions grows a --description /
--description-file / --force / --reason parser section;
RunTaskSetCommand grows the gate check (allow if NotStarted else
require force+reason). Changelog records before/after description.
Tests cover the happy path, the gate-refuses-without-force case,
the force-with-reason path (and that the reason is persisted to
changelog), and the --description-file shell-safety path.

phases[3] - --<field>-append-file (P5 append-only)
Broadest surface touch (every prose-bearing mutation command gains
a sibling --<field>-append-file flag). Concatenation helper lives
in UniPlanFileHelpers or UniPlanOptionParsing; each mutation
handler decides whether to apply a replace (existing --<field>
/ --<field>-file) or an append semantics. Invariant: when both
replace and append flags are set for the same field,
UsageError exit 2 at parse time. Tests cover the happy concat
path (blank-line seam inserted exactly once), the empty-existing-
field case (append acts like replace), the byte-identical shell-
hostile content round-trip, and the replace+append mutual-
exclusion UsageError.

After phases[3] lands, run the full suite, bump kCliVersion to
0.105.0, author v0.105.0 behavior notes in CLAUDE.md + AGENTS.md,
and mirror the notes into square-bot / fie / mm-factory plan-
creation and plan-execution skills.
goals:
- Add --ack-only on every uni-plan mutation command (topic set, phase
  set, lane add, job add, task add, changelog add, verification add,
  manifest add, risk add, next-action add, acceptance-criterion add,
  priority-grouping add, runbook add, residual-risk add, and every
  *set / *remove sibling). Response under --ack-only replaces
  "changes":[{field,old,new}] with "changed_fields":[<names>] on a new
  output schema uni-plan-mutation-ack-v1. Bundle mutation semantics
  unchanged.

- Canonicalize --all-phases on phase readiness, phase metric, and
  phase get. phase metric and phase get already support --phases <csv>;
  --all-phases is sugar meaning "every phase index 0..N-1". phase
  readiness is single-phase today; add a batch loop that emits the
  same per-phase JSON shape in an array.

- Add task set --description and task set --description-file plus a
  safety gate: permitted when task status is not_started; otherwise
  --force --reason <text> is required. Mutation records before/after
  description in changelog. Closes a documented CLI gap where the
  only way to correct a typo in a description was task remove + task
  add (destroying audit history).

- Add --<field>-append-file <path> for every prose field on topic
  set, phase set, phase log, verification add, lane set, testing add,
  risk add/set, next-action add/set, acceptance-criterion add/set,
  priority-grouping add/set, runbook add/set, residual-risk add/set.
  Semantics: read existing field value → concatenate with a blank-line
  seam → file content → write back. Pure token-savings over the
  pull-concat-push scratch-file pattern authors use today.

- Preserve the v0.99.0+ bundle lock + stale-check + atomic rename on
  every mutation, including the new append-file path. Preserve the
  v0.102.0 phase sync-execution semantics. Preserve the v0.101.0
  descendant gates on phase complete and lane complete. Preserve
  the v0.94.0 topic_key_matches_filename validator and every other
  evaluator surface.

- Version bump kCliVersion 0.104.1 → 0.105.0 and document v0.105.0
  behavior notes in CLAUDE.md + AGENTS.md, mirrored into square-bot,
  fie, and mm-factory governance skills where those repos surface the
  flag set to their local agents.
locked_decisions:
- Additive-only CLI surface. No existing flag, schema, evaluator,
  or exit-code semantic changes land in this topic. Every item is
  strictly a new opt-in.

- --ack-only defaults to OFF. uni-plan.ini default-toggle is
  optional and not required in the first landing of phases[0] - may
  be added in a follow-up topic if operators ask for it.

- Response schema rationale: phases[0] emits a NEW schema
  uni-plan-mutation-ack-v1 instead of overloading
  uni-plan-mutation-v1 with optional old/new. This keeps every
  existing parser working unchanged and the new shape discoverable
  by schema-aware consumers.

- --all-phases is sugar for the enumerated --phases 0,1,..,N-1
  form. The canonical underlying argument stays --phases <csv>. If
  both are supplied in the same invocation, UsageError (exit 2) at
  parse time.

- task set --description safety gate mirrors the v0.73.1 phase-set
  completed-phase-timestamp gate shape exactly - same --force /
  --reason pattern, same UsageError path. Not a new gate genre.

- --<field>-append-file seams are exactly one blank line
  (double-newline) between existing content and appended content,
  regardless of trailing whitespace on either side. Empty existing
  content → append acts like replace (no leading blank line).
  --<field>, --<field>-file, and --<field>-append-file are
  mutually exclusive on a single invocation; setting more than one
  at once is UsageError (exit 2).

- Substring-replace rejection is permanent for this topic. Future
  topics may revisit with a different shape (e.g. a separate
  uni-plan patch subcommand), but this bundle does not land any
  substring-level surface.

- Hygiene profile rejection is permanent for this topic. uni-plan
  stays standalone; repo-specific authoring floors live in
  consumer-local hooks.

- No validator changes, no new evaluator IDs, no promotions of
  ErrorMinor/Warning to ErrorMajor, no demotions.

- Version plan: all four phases must complete before bumping
  kCliVersion. Individual phases may commit with an explanatory
  changelog note but the version stays 0.104.1 until phases[3]
  completes. Single MINOR bump 0.104.1 → 0.105.0 at topic seal.

- CLI-only access to .Plan.json bundles is preserved throughout. No
  raw json.load ever touches a bundle file in implementation or
  tests. Test fixtures use uni-plan topic add / phase add / etc.
  to build their scenarios, not hand-written JSON.

- No claude-coauthor trailer on any commit body (per the global rule).

- Pre-1.0 SemVer discipline: this is a MINOR bump, not 1.x.

next_actions.rationale: phases[0] is scoped to --ack-only; phases[1..3] are the remaining CliAgentErgonomics surface additions still to land before the v0.105.0 bump at phases[3] close
next_actions.statement: Execute phases[1] implementation: --all-phases canonicalization across phase readiness/metric/get with new kPhaseReadinessBatchSchema wrapper

non_goals:
- No changes to the .Plan.json bundle file format. Every addition is
  CLI surface only. Existing bundles remain readable by earlier
  uni-plan versions; --ack-only is strictly opt-in and does not alter
  what lands on disk.

- No flag deprecations. Every existing flag continues to work. The
  default mutation response shape (changes[] with old/new) stays
  the default; --ack-only is opt-in.

- No concurrency-model changes. The v0.99.0+ FBundleFileLock +
  FBundleReadSession + GuardedWriteBundle + UPLAN_FAULT_PRE_RENAME
  surface is untouched.

- No evaluator surface changes. The 35 validator checks keep firing
  the same way. No new evaluator IDs. No new ErrorMajor/ErrorMinor/
  Warning promotions or demotions.

- No hygiene profile concept in uni-plan.ini. The proposer's
  min_design_chars / min_solid_words / min_recursive_words /
  min_field_coverage_percent / min_evidence_items / require_*
  thresholds stay in consumer-local hooks (e.g. square-bot's
  plan_board_hygiene.py). See the proposer's Docs/Proposals/
  IMPROVEMENT.Proposal.md P2 - reshaped down for reasons recorded
  in the topic's locked_decisions.

- No write-time hygiene validators (P3 rejected). no_smart_quotes,
  no_dev_absolute_path, no_duplicate_changelog, and every other
  content-hygiene check stay at validate-time only. No new exit code 3.

- No auto-changelog silent dedupe (P6 rejected). The existing
  no_duplicate_changelog warning keeps carrying the evidence signal
  for idempotent-retry mistakes.

- No phase get --field <name> / --fields <csv> narrow reader (P4
  deferred). Reassess after P1 + P5-append land; the shared
  projector refactor is only worth the cost if the patch-heavy use
  case remains painful under the new surface.

- No substring replace on mutation fields (P5 reshaped). --<field>-
  replace-substring-file <from> <to> is out of scope. Consumers use
  phase get (future P4) plus local edit plus phase set append/full
  replacement.

- The 12 call sites must ALL be updated. A missed call site silently
  falls back to the v1 shape under --ack-only, which is a correctness
  bug not a performance regression. Grep for EmitMutationJson\b at
  merge time; expect exactly 12 hits.
- The default argument on the header signature is a migration
  convenience only. At merge time, make every call site explicit
  (no reliance on the default) to surface any future call site that
  forgets to plumb the flag.
- Do not collapse the old/new population inside the caller when
  --ack-only is set. The caller still builds the full Changes
  vector; EmitMutationJson decides what to emit. That keeps the
  control flow uniform across call sites.
- The bundle-bytes-identical test is load-bearing. If it fails, the
  flag has touched the mutation path, which is a correctness bug.
  Fix the plumbing; do not weaken the test.

Next phase
- phases[1] (--all-phases canonicalization) begins after phases[0]
  completes. Do not start phases[1] in parallel with phases[0] work
  on the same branch; they touch disjoint files but a mistaken
  parallel merge can mask each other's test coverage.
phases.investigation:
The uni-plan-mutation-v1 response envelope is built in a single
function, EmitMutationJson, at Source/UniPlanCommandMutationCommon.cpp
lines 14 through 37. Every mutation command in the CLI funnels through
this function. A grep of the symbol across the Source/ tree returns
exactly 12 call sites: 9 in UniPlanCommandMutation.cpp (for the
value-level setters of topic fields, phase fields, job fields, task
fields, lane fields, testing records, file_manifest records,
changelog records, verification records) and 3 in
UniPlanCommandLifecycle.cpp (for the semantic transitions phase start,
phase complete, and the bundle-level topic lifecycle calls).

Every caller builds a std::vector of std::pair<std::string,
std::pair<std::string, std::string>> containing (field_name,
(old_value, new_value)) tuples, then passes it through. The function
renders "changes":[{field, old, new}, ...] into the JSON. That means
the response always echoes both the prior content and the new content
of every field it touched, even when the caller never needed the
prior content. On a dense phase set that replaces every design field,
the response doubles the data volume of the incoming request.

The proposer observed approximately 1.5 to 2 million characters of
pure mutation-echo noise across an 11-phase, 500-CLI-call session in
the square-bot MegaScan bundle. The savings on an --ack-only-enabled
session is approximately 30 to 40 percent of the transcript, per the
proposer's own volumetric estimates. Those estimates were validated
against the actual call pattern in that session and are conservative.

The on-disk bundle content is unaffected by the response shape.
GuardedWriteBundle (v0.99.0+) in Source/UniPlanBundleWriteGuard.cpp
writes the serialized FTopicBundle regardless of what
EmitMutationJson later streams to stdout. The lock-and-stale-check
flow at the mutation funnel preserves the v0.99.0 concurrency
guarantees.

The existing BaseOptions struct in Source/UniPlanOptionTypes.h is the
correct home for the new mbAckOnly flag. It is already shared by
every mutation command, already parsed once in ConsumeCommonOptions
in Source/UniPlanOptionParsing.cpp, and already threaded through to
each Run*Command handler. Every handler passes a reference to its
Options struct when calling EmitMutationJson; extending the signature
to accept a small FMutationEmitOptions parameter or simply passing
the bool directly from the Options struct is purely additive.

The new response schema constant kMutationAckSchema lives in
Source/UniPlanCliConstants.h alongside the existing kMutationSchema.
Emitting a distinct schema name (rather than overloading the v1
schema with optional old/new fields) keeps every existing schema-
aware parser working unchanged. Callers that opt into --ack-only see
a schema they must handle explicitly, which is the right contract.

The flag is strictly opt-in. No uni-plan.ini default-toggle lands in
this phase; that is a potential follow-up, gated on whether operators
actually want repo-level quiet-by-default behavior. Keeping the
default unchanged preserves backward compatibility with every
existing script, skill, and agent that consumes the current shape.

Scope of changes in this phase: one schema constant added, one
BaseOptions field added, one parser line added in
ConsumeCommonOptions, one conditional branch added inside
EmitMutationJson, 12 call sites updated to thread the flag through.
No changes to any validator, any bundle field, any auto-changelog
semantic, any lock-and-stale-check behavior, any exit code, any
Options struct beyond the BaseOptions extension, or any other Emit*
function.

phases.multi_platforming:
Target platforms for this phase: macOS arm64, Windows x64, Linux x86-64.

Shared logic. The mutation surface is the same on every platform.
EmitMutationJson writes to std::cout via iostream operators; the
--ack-only branch is identical on all three platforms. There is no
platform-specific ifdef logic introduced in this phase.

File-system interaction. Zero. The flag changes only stdout
rendering. No file is opened, read, written, or renamed in the
--ack-only path that is not also opened in the default path. The
v0.99.0+ GuardedWriteBundle flow runs identically on every platform.

Line ending considerations. The response envelope ends with a single
`\n` from `std::cout << "\n";`. Terminal rendering is platform-
consistent. JSON parsers on the consuming side tolerate either LF or
CRLF line endings. No new line-ending handling is introduced.

Build discipline. No new translation unit, no new library dependency,
no new ifdef guard. The change lands in existing files:
- Source/UniPlanCliConstants.h (one constant)
- Source/UniPlanOptionTypes.h (one field on BaseOptions)
- Source/UniPlanOptionParsing.cpp (one parser clause)
- Source/UniPlanCommandMutationCommon.h (signature extension with
  default argument)
- Source/UniPlanCommandMutationCommon.cpp (the branch)
- 12 call sites: pass the flag through

Test build. No new test translation unit strictly required; the
existing Test/UniPlanTestMutation.cpp grows new test fixtures. The
gtest harness runs identically on macOS, Windows, and Linux.

Verification command (same on every platform):

    uni-plan phase set --topic SampleTopic --phase 0 \
        --investigation "test" --ack-only

Expected output on every platform is the ack shape:

    {"schema":"uni-plan-mutation-ack-v1","ok":true,
     "topic":"SampleTopic","target":"phases[0]",
     "changed_fields":["investigation"],
     "auto_changelog":true}

Cross-host parity evidence: the Windows and Linux builds emit byte-
identical JSON response bodies to the macOS Debug build on the same
input bundle. This is already the cross-platform invariant for every
mutation response; --ack-only preserves it.

Known platform-specific pitfalls ruled out:

- Path separator: not relevant; no paths emitted in ack shape.
- Line endings on Windows: std::cout respects the stream mode; JSON
  parsers are tolerant. No change from the existing mutation output.
- Locale-dependent formatting: no numeric or date formatting emitted
  in the ack shape beyond what the v1 shape already emits.
- Threading: response rendering is single-threaded from the main
  command handler. No concurrency behavior change.

phases.best_practices:
SOLID and refactor guidance for phases[1]:

Single Responsibility. EmitPhaseReadinessBatch wraps the existing
per-phase renderer with the batch envelope. It does NOT duplicate
the gate-evaluation logic; it calls the same single-phase payload
function that v1 emission uses. The split is payload-vs-envelope,
not parallel implementation.

Open/Closed. Adding --all-phases is strictly additive on the option
struct and parser. No existing parser clause changes meaning; no
existing schema renames.

Domain type over primitive bool. mbAllPhases is a bool member of
the typed Options struct (F-prefix type, mb-prefix member). Not a
magic number, not a string token, not an untyped map entry.

No stringly-typed if-chain. The phase-index selector logic is three
disjoint cases: (a) --phase <N> sets mPhaseIndex, (b) --phases <csv>
populates mPhaseIndices, (c) --all-phases sets mbAllPhases. The
downstream handler branches on the three fields with structured
control flow. No "mode" string tag shared between parser and
handler.

No large enum switches. The batch expansion is one branch (set
mPhaseIndices to 0..N-1). No switch statement grows.

No raw ownership. The Options struct is value-typed; no heap
allocation added. The bundle is loaded once and passed by const
reference to the batch emitter.

No std::regex. Parser tokens are exact-string comparisons, same as
every other clause in UniPlanOptionParsing.cpp.

No goto. Parser uses for-loop + continue; handler uses structured
if/else.

Naming:

    kPhaseReadinessBatchSchema  // constant, k-prefix
    FPhaseReadinessOptions      // struct, F-prefix
    mbAllPhases                 // bool member, mb-prefix
    mPhaseIndex                 // member, m-prefix
    mPhaseIndices               // member, m-prefix
    InBundle                    // input param, In-prefix
    InArgs, OutError            // same
    RunPhaseReadinessCommand    // PascalCase function

Diagnostic discipline. No std::cerr output added in this phase. The
mutual-exclusion error message is returned via OutError to the
handler's existing error-reporting path, which formats it
consistently with every other UsageError.

Testing discipline. New tests cover:

1. Happy path: --all-phases on a 3-phase topic emits a batch array
   with phase_index 0, 1, 2.
2. Empty-topic edge: --all-phases on a zero-phase topic emits an
   empty phases[] array and exits 0.
3. Mutual-exclusion: --all-phases + --phase → UsageError exit 2.
4. Mutual-exclusion: --all-phases + --phases → UsageError exit 2.
5. Equivalence: phase get --phases 0,1,2 emits the same payload
   content as phase get --all-phases on a 3-phase topic (same
   field values, same ordering).

Anti-pattern negative example (guarded, lint must skip):

```cpp
// BAD: REJECTED open-ended mode string. Do not reintroduce.
void RunPhaseReadinessBad(const std::string &InMode,
                          const FPhaseReadinessOptions &InOpts)
{
    if (InMode == "single")        { /* ... */ }
    else if (InMode == "all")      { /* ... */ }
    else if (InMode == "range")    { /* ... */ }
    else if (InMode == "indexed")  { /* ... */ }
    else if (InMode == "custom")   { /* ... */ }
}
```

The accepted shape is three disjoint typed fields on the Options
struct (mPhaseIndex, mPhaseIndices, mbAllPhases). The parser
normalizes them to a single mPhaseIndices vector before handler
dispatch; the handler has one mode-agnostic loop.

phases.investigation:
Three query commands share a "one phase or many" decision today, and
the three take different paths through the CLI.

phase get: single by default; multi via --phases <csv>. The multi
path emits the wrapped uni-plan-phase-get-v2 envelope with a
phases[] array at the top level. Implementation lives in
Source/UniPlanCommandPhase.cpp in the RunPhaseGetCommand handler.

phase metric: same shape. Single by default; multi via --phases
<csv>. Wrapped envelope on the multi path. Implementation adjacent
to RunPhaseGetCommand in Source/UniPlanCommandPhase.cpp.

phase readiness: single only. Required --phase <N>. There is no
batch path. Every caller that wants "is every phase ready" runs a
shell for-loop that forks one uni-plan process per phase. On a
dense 11-phase topic this cost ~30-50ms cold-start per fork, totaling
~350ms for the loop. Implementation lives in
Source/UniPlanCommandSemanticQuery.cpp at RunPhaseReadinessCommand
(near line 264, confirmed by grep at audit time).

The fix is surface-level only. Two parts:

1. Add --all-phases as sugar for "every phase index 0..N-1" on all
three commands. When --all-phases is set, expand internally to the
full index set; the downstream iteration path is the same as the
existing --phases <csv> path on phase get and phase metric. No new
internal code path; the flag expansion happens at parser time.

2. Give phase readiness its own batch iteration. Since phase
readiness does not yet have a wrapped schema, this phase introduces
uni-plan-phase-readiness-batch-v1 in UniPlanCliConstants.h. The
batch schema shape parallels uni-plan-phase-get-v2:

   {"schema":"uni-plan-phase-readiness-batch-v1",
    "generated_utc":"<utc>",
    "repo_root":"<root>",
    "topic":"<T>",
    "phases":[
      {"phase_index":0,"ready":true,"gates":[{...}]},
      {"phase_index":1,"ready":false,"gates":[{...}]},
      ...
    ]}

Individual per-phase objects carry the same fields as today's
uni-plan-phase-readiness-v1 payload. Consumers that already parse
v1 can parse each element of phases[] unchanged.

Mutual exclusion rules (parse-time):

- --phase and --phases: mutually exclusive (existing v0.104.1
  behavior; preserved).
- --phase and --all-phases: mutually exclusive. UsageError exit 2.
- --phases and --all-phases: mutually exclusive. UsageError exit 2.
- None of the three set: single-phase commands default to phase 0;
  list commands emit every phase (existing default; preserved).

Empty-topic edge case: if topic has zero phases, --all-phases emits
an empty phases[] array under the batch schema and exits 0. Callers
that expect at least one phase can check phases[].length themselves;
the CLI does not flag this.

Out of scope for this phase: changing phase metric or phase get
batch schemas; changing any mutation surface; changing any
validator. These three query commands are the only touch points.

phases.multi_platforming:
Target platforms: macOS arm64, Windows x64, Linux x86-64.

Shared logic. The --all-phases surface is identical on every
platform. Option parsing is byte-identical input handling; emission
uses std::cout; the underlying iteration is over a std::vector<int>
mPhaseIndices. No platform-specific ifdef lands in this phase.

File-system. No file opened, read, written, or renamed. Query-only.

Threading. Query commands are single-threaded. No concurrency
concerns.

Cross-platform verification (same on every platform):

    uni-plan phase readiness --topic SampleTopic --all-phases

Expected byte-identical output shape across platforms given an
identical bundle input:

    {"schema":"uni-plan-phase-readiness-batch-v1",
     "generated_utc":"...",
     "repo_root":"...",
     "topic":"SampleTopic",
     "phases":[
       {"phase_index":0,"ready":...,"gates":[...]},
       {"phase_index":1,"ready":...,"gates":[...]},
       {"phase_index":2,"ready":...,"gates":[...]}
     ]}

The generated_utc and repo_root fields vary by execution context;
every other field is identical given an identical bundle.

Known ruled-out pitfalls:

- Path separators: only repo_root carries a path; emitted as-is
  from the bundle discovery logic, platform-appropriate.
- Line endings: JSON output uses \n consistently.
- Locale: no locale-dependent formatting in the batch envelope.
- Case sensitivity: topic keys are already case-sensitive per
  existing bundle contract (v0.94.0 regex).

phases.best_practices:
SOLID and discipline for phases[2]:

Single Responsibility. The gate lives entirely inside
RunTaskSetCommand. The option struct carries the raw flag values.
The parser is unchanged in its error-reporting contract. The gate
has exactly one job: enforce "only forceable descriptions on non-
NotStarted tasks, reason required".

Domain type over primitive. EExecutionStatus drives the gate
decision (not a string compare). mbForce and mbDescriptionSet are
typed booleans on the Options struct. mReason is a typed string,
trimmed and non-empty checked explicitly.

No stringly-typed if-chain. The gate is three disjoint cases:
(a) not_started → apply, (b) non-not_started with valid force+reason
→ apply + auto-changelog with reason, (c) non-not_started without
valid force+reason → refuse. Structured control flow, no string
comparison against status names.

No new raw ownership. Options is a value struct, std::string fields
only. No heap allocation added.

Naming:

    mDescription           // m-prefix member
    mbDescriptionSet       // mb-prefix bool member
    mbForce                // mb-prefix bool member
    mReason                // m-prefix member
    RunTaskSetCommand      // PascalCase

Diagnostic discipline. Error messages via OutError and the existing
diagnostic path. No new std::cerr writes. The gate message includes
the current status to help the caller understand why the refusal
fired.

Testing discipline. Happy path, refusal path, forced path (with
and without reason), and shell-hostile description-file path all
covered. The changelog-embedded-reason invariant is load-bearing;
the test for it ensures the audit trail is preserved.

Anti-pattern negative example (guarded, lint must skip):

```cpp
// BAD: REJECTED stringly-compared status check.
if (Task.mStatus == "not_started") { /* apply */ }
else if (Task.mStatus == "in_progress") { /* refuse */ }
else if (Task.mStatus == "blocked") { /* refuse */ }
// ... etc
```

The accepted shape uses the typed enum:

```cpp
if (Task.mStatus == EExecutionStatus::NotStarted) { /* apply */ }
else { /* gate */ }
```

EExecutionStatus is the single source of truth for task status
semantics; any new terminal/non-terminal check lives there, not in
ad-hoc handler branches.

No deprecation concerns. task add --description keeps working.
task set --status, --evidence, --notes keep working. The new flag
is strictly additive.

phases.investigation:
FTaskRecord.mDescription already exists in Source/UniPlanTaxonomyTypes.h
(around line 34). The field is populated by task add via
FTaskAddOptions.mDescription (Source/UniPlanOptionTypes.h around line
572) and rendered by every task-displaying command. The mutation-side
asymmetry is that FTaskSetOptions (same file, around lines 280 to
289) carries mStatus (as std::optional<EExecutionStatus> opStatus),
mEvidence, and mNotes but not mDescription.

This is the only mutable prose field on FTaskRecord that cannot be
edited after task add. The consequence is documented in the square-
bot cli-gap-discipline.md as a known pain point that forces task
remove plus task add when a typo needs correcting, which destroys
the task's audit history (mEvidence, mNotes, any status transitions
captured in changelog).

The safety-gate shape follows the precedent set in v0.73.1 when
phase set --status completed gained a gate requiring
--started-at <iso> when the phase's mStartedAt was empty. The
pattern is:

1. Detect the constraint at parse time where possible; otherwise
   at handler dispatch (the constraint here depends on reading the
   target task's current status, which requires the bundle to be
   loaded; so the check lives in RunTaskSetCommand).
2. Refuse with UsageError (exit 2) and a diagnostic message when
   the constraint isn't met.
3. Provide --force + --reason as the explicit bypass. The reason
   is required text; --force alone (without --reason) is still
   refused.

The asymmetric precedent point: phase set gate happens to be
parse-time because it depends on a flag combination, not on read
state. RunTaskSetCommand's gate is handler-time because it depends
on Task.mStatus, which must be read from the bundle. Both emit
UsageError exit 2 for consistency with the existing gate vocabulary.

Force path audit trail: when the gate is forced, the auto-changelog
entry embeds both the before/after description text and the
provided reason. This is the same pattern the CLI uses today for
phase cancel (v0.89.0) where the reason text is persisted to
mBlockers and surfaced in the changelog entry. The reason text must
be non-empty after whitespace trim; an empty reason with --force is
treated as "no reason provided" and is refused.

Scope of changes: one Options struct extension, one parser section,
one handler gate, one test suite section. No FTaskRecord change, no
schema change, no validator change, no concurrency change. The
v0.99.0+ lock-and-stale-check flow still protects the write.

phases.best_practices:
SOLID and discipline for phases[3]:

Single Responsibility. Two functions own their corners:
- TryConsumeStringOrFileOrAppendFileOption: option parsing for the
  three companion flags.
- ComputeAppendOrReplace: the concat-or-replace decision given
  existing + new + append-bit.

Every mutation handler's per-field block shrinks to one call of
ComputeAppendOrReplace, removing append-logic drift risk across
30+ call sites.

Open/Closed. Adding a 4th companion flag later (e.g. --<field>-
prepend-file) is a new parser-helper variant plus an enum on
mAppendFields or a new collection on BaseOptions. The current
shape is extensible without rewriting handlers.

Domain type over primitive. The mAppendFields std::set<std::string>
is a typed collection (not an untyped JSON object, not a bit mask).
The field name string is the canonical bundle field name from the
schema (investigation, code_snippets, etc.).

No stringly-typed if-chain. Handler sites use the typed
Options.mBase.mAppendFields.count(FieldName) > 0 query. The
FieldName literal is the same literal used by Changes.emplace_back
and the parser flag registration; it already exists in the code
once per field, so no duplication is introduced.

No large enum switch. Parser dispatch is per-flag-token matching,
as today. Handler dispatch is the existing per-field if-block
structure, extended with one helper call.

No raw ownership. Pure value types throughout. No heap allocation
added. std::set<std::string> is a value type on BaseOptions; small
allocation footprint per invocation.

No std::regex. Parser tokens are exact-string matched against the
flag name.

No goto. Structured control flow.

Naming:

    TryConsumeStringOrFileOrAppendFileOption  // PascalCase fn
    ComputeAppendOrReplace                    // PascalCase fn
    mAppendFields                             // m-prefix member
    InFlagName, InFileFlagName,               // In-prefix params
    InAppendFlagName, InFieldName
    OutValue, OutValueSet, OutAppendFields    // Out-prefix params

Diagnostic discipline. Mutual-exclusion errors surface via OutError
with a message that names the offending field. No std::cerr.

Testing discipline. Load-bearing invariants:

1. Append on empty acts as replace. No leading \n\n.
2. Append on non-empty prepends exactly one \n\n before file
   content.
3. Shell-hostile content round-trips byte-identically.
4. Mutual exclusion blocks two flags for one field at parse time.
5. Cross-field independence: --investigation-append-file and
   --handoff-append-file in the same invocation both land
   correctly.

Anti-pattern negative example (guarded, lint must skip):

```cpp
// BAD: REJECTED per-field mbAppend bool scattered across 30+
// Options structs. Do not reintroduce.
struct FPhaseSetOptions
{
    std::string mInvestigation;
    bool mbInvestigationAppend;
    std::string mCodeEntityContract;
    bool mbCodeEntityContractAppend;
    std::string mCodeSnippets;
    bool mbCodeSnippetsAppend;
    // ... 30 more pairs ...
};
```

The accepted shape is one std::set<std::string> on BaseOptions,
queried by field name at handler dispatch. This:

- Scales to 30+ fields without per-field struct edits.
- Keeps Options structs at their existing size.
- Concentrates the append concern in one place.
- Makes adding new mutable fields mechanical (no struct edits for
  the append story; just the existing parser helper + the existing
  handler block).

Handlers that today route through shared helpers (e.g. AppendPhase*
utilities) should route the append-or-replace decision through
ComputeAppendOrReplace there, so the one-call discipline holds.

phases.investigation:
The v0.76.0 file-based prose input surface added a --<field>-file
<path> sibling for every prose-setting flag, reading raw bytes from
disk so shell-hostile content (backticks, $VAR, quotes, newlines)
round-trips safely. The concrete implementation is
TryConsumeStringOrFileOption in Source/UniPlanOptionParsing.cpp near
line 100, used by every parser in the CLI.

Today that helper supports exactly two input forms per field:
inline --<field> <text> and file-based --<field>-file <path>. Both
apply replace semantics: the handler writes the supplied value as
the new field value, regardless of what the bundle previously held.

For appending content to an existing 9k-char investigation field,
the authoring pattern a dense-plan agent uses today is:

1. uni-plan phase get --topic T --phase N --design  (pull current)
2. Concatenate with local scratch file holding the new content.
3. uni-plan phase set --topic T --phase N --investigation-file X
   (push the concat result).

For a 1k-char semantic addition this means 9k pulled + 10k pushed =
~19k of round-trip traffic for the 1k edit. The proposer estimates
~30-50% savings on iterative-authoring sessions (where small
additions dominate). The savings compounds because every mutation-
echo response (uni-plan-mutation-v1) also echoes 10k of new content,
contributing to the P1 noise class.

The fix is to move the concat to the CLI. Adding a third companion
flag --<field>-append-file <path> with semantics:

- If the target field is empty, append is equivalent to replace
  (no leading seam, just write the file content verbatim).
- If the target field is non-empty, read existing value, append
  \n\n (one blank line), append file content, write result.

The seam is exactly one blank line regardless of trailing whitespace
on the existing value or leading whitespace on the file content.
This normalizes the seam contract and avoids "did the author leave
a trailing newline" ambiguity. Authors who want a different seam
can still use pull-edit-push.

Mutual exclusion: for a given field, a single mutation can set it
only one way. If --investigation AND --investigation-file are both
supplied, today the CLI errors at parse time (verified via grep of
TryConsumeStringOrFileOption). The same discipline extends to
--investigation-append-file: setting any two of the three is a
UsageError exit 2.

Implementation strategy: rather than adding a per-field mbAppend
bit on every Options struct (which would touch 30+ structs), add a
single mAppendFields std::set<std::string> on BaseOptions. The
parser populates it keyed by field name when the append-file flag
is consumed. The handler queries it with
Options.mBase.mAppendFields.count("investigation") > 0 at the
point of applying the field change. One extension point for 30+
fields.

Handler integration: every prose-writing handler already has a
pattern like:

    if (Options.mInvestigationSet)
    {
        Changes.emplace_back("investigation",
            std::make_pair(Bundle.mPhases[Idx].mInvestigation,
                           Options.mInvestigation));
        Bundle.mPhases[Idx].mInvestigation = Options.mInvestigation;
    }

The append-aware variant:

    if (Options.mInvestigationSet)
    {
        std::string NewValue = Options.mInvestigation;
        if (Options.mBase.mAppendFields.count("investigation") > 0)
        {
            const std::string &OldValue =
                Bundle.mPhases[Idx].mInvestigation;
            NewValue = OldValue.empty()
                ? Options.mInvestigation
                : (OldValue + "\n\n" + Options.mInvestigation);
        }
        Changes.emplace_back("investigation",
            std::make_pair(Bundle.mPhases[Idx].mInvestigation, NewValue));
        Bundle.mPhases[Idx].mInvestigation = NewValue;
    }

The lookup is centralizable into a small helper
MaybeAppendToExisting(Field, New, Existing, AppendFields) that
returns the computed NewValue. Every handler call-site becomes a
one-line call instead of an inline branch. This keeps the append
concern in one place and avoids append-logic drift across 30+
handlers.

Scope: every prose-bearing mutation command surface. Specifically:

- topic set: summary, goals, non_goals, problem_statement,
  baseline_audit, execution_strategy, locked_decisions,
  source_references.
- phase set: scope, output, investigation, code_entity_contract,
  code_snippets, best_practices, multi_platforming, readiness_gate,
  handoff.
- phase log: change, affected.
- verification add/set: check, detail, result.
- testing add/set: step, action, expected, evidence.
- lane set: scope, exit_criteria.
- manifest set: description.
- risk add/set: statement, mitigation, rationale, owner.
- next-action add/set: statement, rationale, owner.
- acceptance-criterion add/set: statement, measure.
- priority-grouping add/set: rule, name, description.
- runbook add/set: name, trigger, description.
- residual-risk add/set: statement, observation, why_deferred,
  target_phase, closure_sha, area.

Not a trivial touch set, but the centralized helper makes the diff
per file minimal: one call replaces one inline branch per field.

Invariants preserved:

- Byte-identical bundle content under equivalent pull-concat-push
  manual flow vs --<field>-append-file flow.
- Lock-and-stale-check (v0.99.0+) still protects the write.
- Auto-changelog still stamps one entry per mutation (not two).
- All validators see the same post-mutation bundle content.
- Exit codes unchanged.
- Mutual-exclusion refuses the "two flags for one field" case at
  parse time (exit 2).

phases.multi_platforming:
Target platforms: macOS arm64, Windows x64, Linux x86-64.

Shared logic. String concatenation + std::set operations are
portable stdlib. TryReadFileToString is the existing v0.76.0 helper
used by every --<field>-file; --<field>-append-file reuses it
verbatim.

File-system. Same as --<field>-file semantics: files are opened in
binary mode, slurped byte-identically, no line-ending translation.
A CRLF-terminated file on Windows and an LF-terminated file on
macOS both round-trip exactly as authored; the \n\n seam is inserted
regardless of what trailing whitespace exists in the existing value
or leading whitespace exists in the file content.

Cross-platform verification:

    # macOS / Linux
    printf 'first\n' > /tmp/a.txt
    printf 'second\n' > /tmp/b.txt
    uni-plan phase set --topic T --phase 0 \
        --investigation-file /tmp/a.txt
    uni-plan phase set --topic T --phase 0 \
        --investigation-append-file /tmp/b.txt
    # Expected: investigation == "first\n\n\nsecond\n"
    # (existing "first\n" + "\n\n" seam + "second\n")

    # Windows
    # Same commands through cmd.exe / PowerShell; bytes identical.

Threading. Same as every mutation command: single-threaded through
GuardedWriteBundle. No new concurrency concerns.

Known pitfalls ruled out:

- Path separator inside file paths: CLI accepts platform-native
  paths; no CLI-side translation.
- BOM in files: TryReadFileToString reads bytes verbatim; a file
  with a BOM includes the BOM in the concatenated result. This is
  the same v0.76.0 behavior consumers already expect.
- Locale-dependent comparison: mAppendFields uses byte-level
  std::string compare via std::set; no locale dependency.
phases.output:
- New shared helper TryConsumeStringOrFileOrAppendFileOption in
  Source/UniPlanOptionParsing.cpp that extends
  TryConsumeStringOrFileOption with a third companion flag
  --<field>-append-file <path>. When that flag is consumed, the
  parser sets both the field's target value (the file content) AND
  a per-field mbAppend bit so the handler knows to apply concat
  semantics instead of replace.
- Every Options struct carrying a prose field gains one mb<Field>Append
  bool per prose field OR a single std::set<std::string>
  mAppendFields collection on BaseOptions. The second approach is
  cleaner and scales across 30+ prose fields.
- Every mutation handler that writes a prose field consults
  mAppendFields; if the field name is present, handler reads the
  existing bundle value, computes concat with blank-line seam, and
  writes the result. Otherwise existing replace semantics.
- Mutual-exclusion enforcement at parse time: setting two or more
  of --<field>, --<field>-file, --<field>-append-file for the same
  field raises UsageError exit 2 with a clear diagnostic.
- Tests cover happy concat (non-empty existing + non-empty append),
  empty-existing-becomes-replace, shell-hostile round-trip,
  mutual-exclusion refusal, and the cross-field no-interference
  invariant (two append flags on different fields in one command
  both land correctly).

Semantics: read the existing value of the target field from the
bundle, insert a blank-line seam (\n\n), concatenate the file's
bytes, write the result back as the new field value. If the
existing field is empty, the append acts like a pure replace (no
leading blank line). Byte-identical round-trip of shell-hostile
content from the input file through to the persisted bundle.

Mutual exclusion: --<field>, --<field>-file, and --<field>-append-
file are mutually exclusive on a single invocation. Setting more
than one for the same field is a UsageError (exit 2) at parse time.

problem_statement:
Dense plan authoring by AI agents at scale surfaced four classes of
token + wall-clock waste in the current uni-plan CLI surface.

First, every mutation command echoes the full prior and new value of
every changed field in its response envelope. On the driving
MegaScan session (11 phases, ~381k design chars) the proposer
estimates ~1.5-2M chars of pure mutation-echo noise across the
transcript - for data the caller already had or could trivially
re-fetch via phase get. An opt-in --ack-only flag eliminates this
without touching bundle semantics.

Second, per-phase operations on readiness / metric / get currently
require either a shell for-loop (one process fork per phase, ~30-
50ms cold-start each) or the --phases <csv> enumeration. A
--all-phases sugar form closes both gaps and normalizes the batch
pattern across the three query surfaces.

Third, task description correction currently has no CLI path.
task set exposes status, evidence, and notes but not description;
the documented workaround is task remove + task add, which destroys
audit history. Authors avoid the fix, the typo stays, or the
history gets blown away. A gated task set --description closes the
gap cleanly.

Fourth, appending content to an existing prose field (investigation,
code_entity_contract, best_practices, etc.) requires the author to
phase get the current value, concat locally, and phase set the
result. For a 1k-char addition to a 9k-char field that means ~18k of
round-trip traffic for a semantic 1k-char edit. A --<field>-append-
file variant computes the concatenation server-side.

None of the four classes require bundle format changes, evaluator
changes, or concurrency-model changes. Every change is observable as
a new flag or a new response schema opt-in - fully backward
compatible with existing callers.

summary:
CLI ergonomics improvements for AI-agent dense-plan authoring.

Driven by observed pain in an 11-phase, ~381k-char plan authoring session
that issued ~500 uni-plan invocations from a peer AI agent in the
square-bot repo. The session surfaced 8 ergonomics proposals (P1..P8)
captured in Docs/Proposals/IMPROVEMENT.Proposal.md. This topic implements
the subset accepted by uni-plan maintainer review: P1 (--ack-only
mutation responses), P7 (--all-phases batch for readiness/metric/get),
P8 (task set --description with safety gate), and P5 (--<field>-append-
file variant, append only - substring-replace rejected).

P2 reshaped to drop the .ini profile concept (would couple uni-plan's
validator surface to one consumer's authoring policy) and keep only
validate --all-phases; that sub-item is deferred to a follow-up topic.
P3 rejected: write-time hygiene gates mix validate-time and mutation-
time responsibilities and add a new exit code to the public contract.
P6 rejected: auto-changelog silent dedupe reduces audit-trail signal.
P4 deferred: narrow field reader requires a medium refactor of phase
get emission and is reassessable after P1+P5 land.

Every phase is purely additive on the CLI surface. No bundle file format
changes. No existing flag deprecations. The v0.99.0+ lock-and-stale-
check concurrency model is untouched. Version bump v0.104.1 → v0.105.0
(MINOR per pre-1.0 SemVer; all four phases land together).
